ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Chicken meat dish) is the closest available parent; no specific Murgh Palak or spinach-chicken curry code exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Murgh Palak is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of chicken pieces in spinach-based gravy, usually accompanied by naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry with vegetables, home-prepared — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not list Murgh Palak as a standalone entry. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry with vegetables (per 100 g cooked) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked chicken in curry sauce with spinach (FDC ID 2103689 and related composites). Spinach contribution to micronutrients (iron, vitamin A, vitamin C, folate) is incorporated from ICMR-NIN spinach cooked values. Cream contribution is reflected in saturated fat and energy. All values represent a typical home-style or restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat and vegetable curry dishes — analogous estimate based on chicken curry (GI ~35) from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Murgh Palak. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy with chicken curry (GI ~35, low category) as reported in the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The low GI is consistent with the dish's low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g/100 g), high protein, and the presence of spinach and spices which do not contribute significant glycemic carbohydrates. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 12.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.4 (rounded).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~930 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of portion size as a 300 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 930 mg sodium (~40% of the 2300 mg daily limit); requesting reduced salt preparation or limiting portion to 150 g can help manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Murgh Palak has a low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate density, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with a low-GI accompaniment such as basmati rice or whole-wheat roti, and when cream content is minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream and cooking oil (~2.1 g saturated fat/100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request preparations with reduced cream and oil, as a full 300 g serving provides approximately 6.3 g saturated fat; the dish's spinach content contributes beneficial folate, potassium, and antioxidants that may partially offset cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murgh Palak (also known as Palak Chicken or Chicken Saag) is a classic North Indian curry originating from Punjab, consisting of tender chicken pieces cooked in a vibrant, velvety spinach-based gravy. The dish is prepared by first sautéing onions, garlic, and ginger in oil, then adding whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala, followed by tomatoes and blanched pureed spinach. Chicken is braised in this aromatic green gravy until fully cooked, and a small amount of cream or yoghurt is often stirred in at the end to add richness and balance the slight bitterness of the spinach. The result is a dish with a deep earthy flavour, mild to medium spice heat, and a smooth, thick texture. Murgh Palak is nutritionally notable for its high protein content (~11 g/100 g from chicken), meaningful iron and folate contributions from spinach, and a low glycemic index (~35), making it one of the more nutritionally balanced Indian restaurant curries. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations (~310 mg/100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-compatible. It is commonly served with naan, roti, or basmati rice and is a staple on Indian restaurant menus across Singapore, the UK, and the broader South Asian diaspora.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken spinach curry?
Is murgh palak good for diabetics?
What is the difference between murgh palak and chicken saag?
Is murgh palak high in sodium?
What nutrients does spinach chicken curry provide?
What is the GI of chicken curry?
meal_context: served with naan or basmati rice

ingredient_synonyms.saag: leafy greens, often spinach or mustard greens (Punjabi/Hindi)
ingredient_synonyms.ghee: clarified butter (sometimes used instead of oil)

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Murgh Palak as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry with vegetables and USDA FoodData Central composite entries for chicken in spinach-based curry, with spinach micronutrient contributions incorporated separately. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition of Murgh Palak is well-defined and the source adaptation is methodologically sound.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (chicken curry with vegetables) and USDA FoodData Central composite data; no single approved-source entry covers Murgh Palak exactly. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA composite sources used for this dish. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on chicken curry GI values from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Murgh Palak was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Sodium value (~310 mg/100 g) reflects a typical restaurant preparation; home preparations may be lower. 6. Cream is listed as a dairy allergen; some preparations substitute yoghurt or omit dairy entirely. 7. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g/100 g reflecting trace amounts from cooking oil; this is not confirmed zero. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fatty acid profiles for similar chicken curry dishes and are not directly reported in ICMR-NIN.
